name: NANOGrav pulsar-timing-array stochastic gravitational-wave background
summary: NANOGrav's 15-year pulsar-timing data set (2023) detected a stochastic gravitational-wave background at ~3.5-4 sigma significance, with the inter-pulsar correlation pattern following Hellings-Downs as expected for a true GW background. The strain amplitude (~2.4e-15 at 1/yr) and ~ f^-2/3 spectrum are consistent with supermassive black-hole binary inspirals but also allow exotic origins (cosmic strings, first-order phase transitions, inflation tensor modes, dark-matter substructure). The puzzle is which origin the signal actually has.

characteristic_numbers.note: Median + 90% CI. f^-2/3 spectrum fit; spectral-index posterior is broad enough to admit cosmological sources alongside SMBHB.
Strong preference for correlated GWB over independent pulsar noise.

principles_stressed.note: Doesn't violate any principle but tests our understanding of the nanohertz universe. Astrophysical (SMBHB) and cosmological (cosmic strings, phase transitions, inflation) sources are both viable; spectral-shape and anisotropy measurements over the next 5-10 years should discriminate.

history: Theoretical prediction of SMBHB GW background: Sesana et al. (2008); review Burke-Spolaor et al. (2019). NANOGrav 11-yr (2018) saw common-spectrum hint without Hellings-Downs. NANOGrav 12.5-yr (2020) strengthened. NANOGrav 15-yr (2023), 'Evidence for a gravitational-wave background', ApJL 951, L8, arXiv:2306.16213, achieved Hellings-Downs detection. EPTA, PPTA, CPTA simultaneously announced consistent signals.

references.citation: NANOGrav Collaboration (Agazie et al.), 'The NANOGrav 15-year data set: evidence for a gravitational-wave background', ApJL 951, L8 (2023)
